title-group.article-title: Investigation and Execution of a Multi-Input-Port and Single Output Bidirectional DC-DC Converter for Electric Vehicles Hybrid Energy System

abstract.p: This research proposes a revolutionary multi-input-port bidirectional DC-DC converter for electric vehicle (EV) energy storage. The suggested converter can be utilized in both step-up (boost) and step-down (buck) modes. The suggested arrangement contains three ports 2 (2 input port and 1 output port) so that energy may move between them. Its main characteristics are minimal losses in the suggested converter, low component count, and low peak voltage of the primary switch. Additionaly, by using only two power switches, the proposed converter enables simple power switching between the sources. The suggested converter is suitable for energy storage systems (ESS) since it can operate on both step-up and step-down principles. Therefore, it is possible to load and unload the ESS using a high voltage conversion ratio. Additionally, the recommended converter's small component count allows it to function in both directions, which lowers the system's overall cost.

body.p:
Nomenclature
$\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{H}}$
High side capacitor $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{L}}$
Low side capacitor $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{H}}$
Voltage of high voltage port $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{L}}$
Voltage of low voltage port $\mathrm{V}_{2}$
The second source Voltage L
Inductor $\mathrm{L}_{2}$
Inductor 2 $\mathrm{S}_{2}$
Switch 2 $\mathrm{S}_{1}$
Switch 1 $\mathrm{L}_{1}$
Inductor 1 $\mathrm{L}_{2}$
Inductor 2 C
Capacitor

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: A type of power electronics converter known as a bidirectional DC-DC converter with several ports enables the integration of many energy sources and loads into a hybrid energy system. Energy can be transferred between these types of converters [1]–[4]. It has a wide range of applications since it may be applied to different sources of DC voltage and loads. Hybrid energy systems use multi-port converters to integrate several sources with varying voltage and power ranges [5]–[6]. For example, these converters split energy among sources under various operating conditions and give the necessary load power at the proper voltage level in electric vehicles and energy storage systems electric automobiles. The bidirectional DC-DC converter is one type of converter that may transfer energy flow between the sources of energy and the load [7]. In order to achieve the criteria for load changes in HES, batteries and other ESS are required. In an ESS, power may be transferred from the power source to the DC link using bidirectional DC-DC converters. The ESS's capacity to recover energy depends on these converters [8]. Traditional bidirectional buck-boost converters are one type of bidirectional DC-DC converter. These converters should not be used with HES due to their poor voltage conversion ratio. These converters' semiconductors are also under a lot of voltage stress, which increases the converter's losses [9]–[10]. Isolated and non-isolated multiport converters fall into two different types when it comes to bidirectional capability. High voltage conversion ratios may be obtained by isolated structures by altering the transformer conversion ratio [11]. These converters are more expensive and have higher conduction losses due to their large component count. Moreover, the regulation of different types of converters is complex. Compared to isolated converters, non-isolated converters have a number of benefits, including low cost, simple building, good efficiency at low semiconductor voltage, high voltage conversion ratio, and low electromagnetic interference (EMI) [12-14]

list-item.p: The objective of this project is to build an open loop hardware prototype that boosts voltage From 24 V and 12 V to 69 V using the charging mode and bucking the voltage from 69 V and 12 V to 33 V using the discharging mode. The planned power outputs for this prototype are 149 W in boost mode and 49 W in buck mode.
To achieve a high voltage conversion ratio in both the buck and boost modes.
Power transferred to the load in the bidirectional feature

sec.title: Operation Mode Analysis
sec.p:
This construction is advised with two switches (Sw1 and $\text{Sw}_{\mathrm{w}}$), three capacitors ($\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{L}}$, and $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{H}}$), and two inductors $\left(\mathrm{L}_{1}\right.$ and $\left.\mathrm{L}_{2}\right)$. Fig. 1 illustrates this
Fig. 1.1
Suggested arrangement for the converter circuit

sec.title: Step-Up (Boost) Operation
sec.p: Referenced to fig 1.1
Mode 1:
involves turning both Sw1 and Sw2 ON and OFF. Inductor $\mathrm{L}_{1}$ is magnetized by $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{L}}$, and current across it increases linearly.
Mode 2:

Switches for electricity During mode 2, $\mathrm{S}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{S}_{1}$ are turned ON and OFF, respectively. Currents in $\mathrm{L}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{L}_{2}$ are lowered linearly. In addition to receiving the energy of $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{L}}$, capacitors $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{H}}$ and $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}$ also store energy from $\mathrm{L}_{1}$.

sec.title: Step-Down (Buck) Operation
sec.p: Referenced to fig 1.1
Mode 1:

Sw1 must be turned OFF and Sw2 must be turned ON for Mode 1. $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{H}}$ supplies the energy to $\mathrm{V}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{L}}$. Magnetizing inductors $\mathrm{L}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{L}_{2}$ are seeing an increase in current flow.
Mode 2:
This mode starts when power switches $\mathrm{S}_{w} 1$ and $\mathrm{S}_{w} 2$ are turned ON and OFF, respectively. This mode results in the discharge of capacitors C and $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{L}}$.

sec.label: 2.1.A
sec.title: Mode 1 of Step-Up Operation
sec.p: Fundamental representation of state space model

sec.label: 2.1.B
sec.title: Mode II of Step-Up Operation

disp-formula.tex-math: \begin{equation*} \begin{bmatrix} x\prime_1 \\ x\prime_2 \\ x\prime_4 \\ x\prime_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & -1/L_1 & 1/L_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & -1/L_2 \\ 1/C_H & 0 & (-1)/(R_H \ast C_H) & 0 \\ -1/C & 1/C & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_{L_1} \\ i_{L_2} \\ V_{C_H} \\ V_C \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1/L_1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} V_L + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1/L_2 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} V_2 \end{equation*}

sec.title: Buck Mode
Open Loop Simulation Results of Bidirectional Converter in Boost Mode
sec.p:
The open-loop simulation means performance without any feedback control. Key aspects
•
Voltage Boosting Capability: The ability of the converter to increase the input voltage to a higher output voltage is demonstrated by the simulation. For applications that need energy transfer from a lower voltage source to a higher voltage demand. •
Analysis of Waveform: To comprehend the converter's dynamic behaviour, including ripple, the waveforms of voltage and current are examined. •
Input and Output Voltage: The input voltage of 24 V is stepped up to 69 v. The low voltage side (12 V) indicates the converter's potential application in systems like battery charging/discharging or DC microgrids. •
Switching Frequency: A popular option for balancing efficiency and reducing the size of passive components is a switching frequency of 50 kHz. Smoother waveforms and less electromagnetic interference are two benefits of this frequency. Smaller components might be used with a greater switching frequency, but switching losses could rise. •
Power Output: The converter handles a power level of 149 W, indicative application such as in renewable energy systems, electric vehicles, or backup power supplies. •
Inductor and Capacitors: During the boost phase, the $350 \mu \mathrm{H}$ inductor serves as an energy storage component. It is essential for controlling current ripples and guaranteeing steady functioning. A steady DC output, dynamic responsiveness, and voltage ripple filtering are the functions of the $350 \mu ~\mathrm{F}, 350 \mu ~\mathrm{F}$, and $100 \mu ~\mathrm{F}$ capacitors. An intermediate capacitor ($\mathrm{C}=100 \mu \mathrm{F}$) lowers voltage ripple across the converter and stabilizes switching transients.. •
Load Resistance: The load resistance of 32.6Ω represents the load on the high voltage side, ensuring that the current and voltage are within the operating limits of the circuit. •
Ripple Factors: The voltage and current ripples of 20 % and 10 % respectively are reasonable for an open-loop design. •
Applications: simulation are applied to various fields, including renewable energy systems, electric vehicles, and energy storage systems. •
Component Stress Evaluation: The simulation results reveal the stress on components such as MOSFETs, diodes, inductors, and capacitors.
Fig:3.1
Output current in discharging mode.
Fig:3.2
$\mathrm{v}_{\text {gate-source }}$ waveforms
Fig:3.3
Output voltage vo with load change in boost mode
Fig:3.4
Current waveforms through the inductors $\mathrm{l}_{1}$ in boost mode
Fig:3.5
Voltage waveforms across switches and capacitor in boost mode

sec.title: Open Loop Simulation Results of Bidirectional Converter in Buck Mode

inline-formula.tex-math: $V_{H}=69 ~\mathrm{V}, \quad V_{2}=12 ~\mathrm{V}, \quad f_{s}=50 \text{kHz}, D_{1}=0.4, D_{2}=0.6, R =23 \Omega L_{1}=L_{2}=0.350 \text{mH}, C_{L}=C_{H}=350 \mu F, \quad C=100 \mu F$

list-item.p: Load Resistance (R): 23Ω. represents the equivalent resistance of the load connected on the low-voltage side. This resistance determines output current power.

Efficiency Considerations: Efficiency is generally affected by switching losses, conduction losses in MOSFETs or IGBTs, and resistive losses in the passive components. In an open-loop system, achieving optimal efficiency might require iterative adjustments to the duty cycles and the components.

caption.p: The dynamic result of suggested converter for load change in buck mode
Current through the inductors $\mathrm{l}_{2}$ in buck mode

sec.title: Closed loop MATLAB Simulation Results in Boost Mode

caption.p: Output voltage vo (with input voltage change)
Output voltage vo in discharging mode

sec.title: Closed Loop Simulation Results of Buck Mode

list-item.p: 24 volt to 69 volt at 50 KHz and Delivering 2.15 A load Current.N27ferrite core is assumed.
So, EE/42/21/15 of Core is selected.

sec.title: Selection of Components
sec.p: Component Specification:

Switch used: based on the maximum voltage and current levels obtained, the switches are selected: - $\text{Sw}_{1}$: IRFP260N ($250 ~\mathrm{V} / 43 ~\mathrm{A}$), $\text{Sw}_{2}$: IRFP260N ($250 ~\mathrm{V} / 43 ~\mathrm{A}$)
Capacitor used: based on the maximum voltage and current levels obtained, the capacitors are selected: - $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{H}}$: Electrolytic Capacitor $(200 ~\mathrm{V} / 10 ~\mathrm{A})$
C: Electrolytic Capacitor $(100 ~\mathrm{V} / 5 ~\mathrm{A})$

sec.title: Open Loop Hardware Result
sec.p:
BOOST MODE:
Fig:4.3
Gate driver output (gate pulse)
Fig 4.4
Output voltage for discharging mode
Fig 4.5
Load current for discharging mode
Fig:4.6
Inductor current ($\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{l} 1}$) in discharging mode
Fig:4.7
Load current for charging mode
Fig:4.8
Inductor current $\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{l} 1}$ in charging mode.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The objective of the research was to evaluate and implement a bidirectional converter with many ports that is suggested for HES applications that need energy storage. Using the Simulink model, a bidirectional DC/DC converter with several inputs and one output has been simulated in both charging and discharging modes. When the charging mode is engaged, both the 12 V and 69 V high voltage sides is buck-ed to 24 V, and when the discharging mode is engaged, the low voltage inputs of 24 V and 12 V are boosted to 69 V. Controller design and control methods are explained in detail. The simulation results clearly show that a mode that charges and discharges in both directions may be implemented.

Benefits of the created bidirectional converter
•
It can operate in both step-up and step-down modes in a bidirectional manner. •
It has a simple structure and few components. •
Less voltage stress is present between the switches. •
The simulation demonstrates a functional design, capable of boosting voltage efficiently with acceptable ripple levels. •
The choice of components appears well-matched for the given power and frequency parameters. •
In an open-loop system, variations in input voltage or load could cause fluctuations in output. Implementing feedback control in a closed-loop system would enhance stability and accuracy.

ref.mixed-citation: F. Akar, Y. Tavlasoglu, E. Ugur, B. Vural, I. Aksoy, “A Bidirectional Non-isolated Multi-Input DC-DC Converter for Hybrid Energy Storage Systems in Electric Vehicles,” IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology,2016, vol. 65, no. 10, pp. 7944–7955,10.1109/TVT.2015.2500683.

R.R. Ahrabi, H. Ardi, M. Elmi, A. Ajami, “A Novel Step-Up Multi-input DC-DC Converter for Hybrid Electric Vehicles Application,” IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics,2017, vol. 32, no. 5, pp. 3549–3561, 10.1109/TPEL.2016.2585044.

K. Varesi, S. Hosseini Hosseini, M. Sabahi, E. Babaei, S. Saeidabadi, N. Vosoughi, “Design and Analysis of a Developed Multiport High Step-Up DC-DC Converter,” IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics,2019, vol. 34, no. 6, pp. 5464–5475,10.1109/TPEL.2018.2866492.

Kardan F., R. Alizadeh, M.R. Banaei, “A New Three Input DC/DC Converter for Hybrid PV/FC/Battery Applications,” IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics,2017, vol. 5, no. 4, pp. 1771–1778,10.1109/JESTPE.2017.2731816.

F. Nejabatkhah, S. Danyali, S. H. Hosseini, M. Sabahi, S.M. Niapour, “Modelling and Control of a New Three-Input DC-DC Boost Converter,” IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics,2012, vol. 27, no. 5, pp. 2309–2324,10.1109/TPEL.2011.2172465.

J. Zeng, X. Du, Z. Yang, “A Multiport Bidirectional DC-DC Converter for Hybrid Renewable Energy System Integration,” IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics,2021,vol. 36, no. 11, pp. 12281–12291,10.1109/TPEL.2021.3082427.
Wei Yi a, Hengrui Ma b, Sicheng Peng c, Dichen Liu c, Ziad M. Ali d, e Udaya Dampage f, Ali Hajjiah g, “Analysis and Implementation of Multi-Port Bidirectional Converter for Hybrid Energy Systems”.
Deihimi, A., Mahmoodieh, M.E.S., Iravani, R.,A New Multi-Input Step-Up DC-DC Converter for Hybrid Energy Systems,Electrical Power Systems Research,2017, vol. 149, pp. 111–124 (2017) .

G. Aruna, D. Srinivas Rao, “A New Multilevel Inverter Topology for High Step-Up DC-DC Converter,” Int. J. Sci. Eng. Adv. Technol. , Jun. 2017. [Online]. Available: https://ojs.www.ijseat.com/article/900
Wei Yi Hengrui Ma, Sicheng Peng, Dichen Liu,,Analysis and Implementation of Multi-Port Bidirectional Converter,Energy Reports,2022, vol. 8, pp. 1538–1549,10.1016/j.egyr.2021.12.068.

S.B. Santra, D. Chatterjee, T. -J. Liang, “High Gain and High-Efficiency Bidirectional DC-DC Converter,” IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics,vol. 36, no. 11, pp. 12819–12833, Nov. 2021, doi: 10.1109/TPEL.2021.3077584. keywords: {Inductors; Switches; Topology; Capacitors; Stress; Windings; Soft switching;Bidirectional dc-dc converter; coupled inductor},.
Xiao, Y., Duraij, M., Zhang, Z., Zsurzsan, G., Andersen, M.A.E.,ZVS Design in Full-SiC Three-Level Converter,IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics, 70(9), 8970–8978,10.1109/TIE.2022.3206704.
